Scatterplots, Smoothing, And Groups

Goal

This notebook shows how to build an association plot step by step.

We use the relationship between country income and infant mortality as an example in this notebook.

Analytic Questions

  • How are per-capita income and infant mortality associated across countries?
  • Why can a transformed scale make an association easier to see?
  • What does a smoother add, and what does it hide?
  • How can grouping by region help or overload the plot?

Load Data

countries <- readr::read_csv("data/real/infant_mortality_countries.csv")
Rows: 105 Columns: 6
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (3): country_name, region, oil_exporting_country
dbl (2): income_usd_per_capita, infant_mortality_per_1000
lgl (1): oil_exporter

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

Prepare Data

Each row is one country.

Data preparation step Functions in this chunk
Keep or remove rows filter()
Choose or rename columns select() 
country_data <- countries |>
  filter(
    !is.na(income_usd_per_capita),
    !is.na(infant_mortality_per_1000),
    income_usd_per_capita > 0
  )

country_data |>
  select(country_name, region, income_usd_per_capita, infant_mortality_per_1000) |>
  head(10)
# A tibble: 10 × 4
   country_name             region income_usd_per_capita infant_mortality_per_…¹
   <chr>                    <chr>                  <dbl>                   <dbl>
 1 Algeria                  Africa                   400                    86.3
 2 Burundi                  Africa                    68                   150  
 3 Cameroon                 Africa                   100                   137  
 4 Central.African.Republic Africa                   122                   190  
 5 Chad                     Africa                    70                   160  
 6 Congo                    Africa                   281                   180  
 7 Dahomey                  Africa                    81                   110. 
 8 Egypt                    Africa                   210                   114  
 9 Ethiopia                 Africa                    79                    84.2
10 Ghana                    Africa                   217                    63.7
# ℹ abbreviated name: ¹​infant_mortality_per_1000

Basic Scatterplot

A scatterplot shows two numeric variables at once.

Plot component Code in this chunk
Aesthetic mappings x; y
Geoms / stats geom_point()
geom_point() properties alpha 
p1 <- ggplot(
  country_data,
  aes(x = income_usd_per_capita, y = infant_mortality_per_1000)
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7)

p1
Plot component Code in this chunk
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels labs(); theme_minimal() 
p2 <- p1 +
  labs(
    title = "Income And Infant Mortality Across Countries",
    x = "Per-capita income, U.S. dollars",
    y = "Infant mortality per 1,000 live births"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p2

Countries with higher per-capita income tend to have lower infant mortality, but the raw income scale compresses many lower-income countries into the left side of the plot.

Use A Log Income Scale

Income is positive and right-skewed. A log scale is typically appropriate for income and makes the associations easier to inspect. Note that this transforms the figure only and not the underlying data.

Plot component Code in this chunk
Aesthetic mappings x; y
Geoms / stats geom_point()
geom_point() properties alpha
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels scale_x_log10() 
p1 <- ggplot(
  country_data,
  aes(x = income_usd_per_capita, y = infant_mortality_per_1000)
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::label_dollar())

p1
Plot component Code in this chunk
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels labs(); theme_minimal() 
p2 <- p1 +
  labs(
    title = "Income And Infant Mortality Across Countries",
    x = "Per-capita income, U.S. dollars, log scale",
    y = "Infant mortality per 1,000 live births"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p2

The data are the same. The scale changed. On the log scale, equal spacing means equal ratios rather than equal dollar differences.

Add A Smoother

A smoother summarizes the pattern.

geom_smooth() can use different methods. If we do not name the method, ggplot2 chooses one for us. Here we name method = "loess" so students can see exactly what is being drawn.

Plot component Code in this chunk
Aesthetic mappings x; y
Geoms / stats geom_point(); geom_smooth()
geom_point() properties alpha
geom_smooth() properties se; color; linewidth
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels scale_x_log10() 
p1 <- ggplot(
  country_data,
  aes(x = income_usd_per_capita, y = infant_mortality_per_1000)
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.55) +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, color = "steelblue", linewidth = 1) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::label_dollar())

p1
`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'
Plot component Code in this chunk
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels labs(); theme_minimal() 
p2 <- p1 +
  labs(
    title = "A Smoother Summarizes The Association",
    x = "Per-capita income, U.S. dollars, log scale",
    y = "Infant mortality per 1,000 live births"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p2
`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'

The warning shows you that the smoother is defaulting to loess. The loess smoother follows local curvature.

Compare Smoother Types

Different smoothers answer different visual questions.

  • method = "loess" draws a flexible local smoother.
  • method = "gam" draws a flexible model-based smoother. GAM stands for “generalized additive model”.
  • method = "lm" draws one straight-line trend from a linear model.

First, look at each smoother separately.

Plot component Code in this chunk
Aesthetic mappings x; y
Geoms / stats geom_point(); geom_smooth()
geom_point() properties alpha
geom_smooth() properties method = "loess"; se; color; linewidth
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels scale_x_log10(); labs(); theme_minimal() 
ggplot(
  country_data,
  aes(x = income_usd_per_capita, y = infant_mortality_per_1000)
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.35) +
  geom_smooth(
    method = "loess",
    se = FALSE,
    color = "steelblue",
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::label_dollar()) +
  labs(
    title = "Loess Smoother",
    subtitle = "Flexible local smoother",
    x = "Per-capita income, U.S. dollars, log scale",
    y = "Infant mortality per 1,000 live births"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
Plot component Code in this chunk
geom_smooth() properties method = "gam"; formula; se; color; linewidth 
ggplot(
  country_data,
  aes(x = income_usd_per_capita, y = infant_mortality_per_1000)
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.35) +
  geom_smooth(
    method = "gam",
    formula = y ~ s(x, bs = "cs"),
    se = FALSE,
    color = "darkorange",
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::label_dollar()) +
  labs(
    title = "GAM Smoother",
    subtitle = "Flexible model-based smoother",
    x = "Per-capita income, U.S. dollars, log scale",
    y = "Infant mortality per 1,000 live births"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Generalized Additive Models are powerful, but complex and less commonly used. They perform very well but reporting them adds an explanatory burden, so beware.

Plot component Code in this chunk
geom_smooth() properties method = "lm"; se; color; linewidth 
ggplot(
  country_data,
  aes(x = income_usd_per_capita, y = infant_mortality_per_1000)
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.35) +
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    se = FALSE,
    color = "firebrick",
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::label_dollar()) +
  labs(
    title = "Linear Model Smoother",
    subtitle = "One straight-line trend",
    x = "Per-capita income, U.S. dollars, log scale",
    y = "Infant mortality per 1,000 live births"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Now compare them on the same plot.

Plot component Code in this chunk
Aesthetic mappings x; y; color
geom_smooth() properties (layer 1) method = "loess"; se; linewidth
geom_smooth() properties (layer 2) method = "gam"; formula; se; linewidth; linetype
geom_smooth() properties (layer 3) method = "lm"; se; linewidth; linetype
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels scale_x_log10(); scale_color_manual(); labs(); theme_minimal() 
ggplot(
  country_data,
  aes(x = income_usd_per_capita, y = infant_mortality_per_1000)
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.25) +
  geom_smooth(
    aes(color = "Loess"),
    method = "loess",
    se = FALSE,
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  geom_smooth(
    aes(color = "GAM"),
    method = "gam",
    formula = y ~ s(x, bs = "cs"),
    se = FALSE,
    linewidth = 1,
    linetype = "dashed"
  ) +
  geom_smooth(
    aes(color = "Linear model"),
    method = "lm",
    se = FALSE,
    linewidth = 1,
    linetype = "dotted"
  ) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::label_dollar()) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "Loess" = "steelblue",
      "GAM" = "darkorange",
      "Linear model" = "firebrick"
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Three Smoothers Show Different Summaries",
    subtitle = "Loess and GAM can bend; a linear model shows one straight-line trend",
    x = "Per-capita income, U.S. dollars, log scale",
    y = "Infant mortality per 1,000 live births",
    color = "Smoother"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

The loess and GAM lines can bend with the data. The linear model line is easier to summarize, but it can miss curvature.

Check Groups Before Coloring

Before adding a grouping variable, check how many observations are in each group.

Data preparation step Functions in this chunk
Count or summarize count() 
country_data |>
  count(region, sort = TRUE)
# A tibble: 4 × 2
  region       n
  <chr>    <int>
1 Africa      34
2 Asia        27
3 Americas    22
4 Europe      18

Small groups can make group-specific patterns look more stable than they are.

Add Region With Color

Color can show whether regional clusters are present.

Plot component Code in this chunk
Geoms / stats geom_point()
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels scale_x_log10() 
p1 <- ggplot(
  country_data,
  aes(
    x = income_usd_per_capita,
    y = infant_mortality_per_1000,
    color = region
  )
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.75) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::label_dollar())

p1
Plot component Code in this chunk
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels labs(); theme_minimal() 
p2 <- p1 +
  labs(
    title = "Income And Infant Mortality By Region",
    x = "Per-capita income, U.S. dollars, log scale",
    y = "Infant mortality per 1,000 live births",
    color = "Region"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p2

Grouping helps us see regional clustering. It also adds visual complexity and can be hard to interpret, so be careful. If this is an important plot, you will likely want to make sure the colors are visually separable for people with vision impairments. We’ll discuss this later.

Facet When Color Gets Busy

Facets split one busy plot into repeated smaller plots.

Plot component Code in this chunk
Aesthetic mappings x; y
Geoms / stats geom_point()
geom_point() properties alpha; color
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels scale_x_log10(); facet_wrap(); labs(); theme_minimal() 
ggplot(
  country_data,
  aes(x = income_usd_per_capita, y = infant_mortality_per_1000)
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, color = "steelblue") +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::label_dollar()) +
  facet_wrap(~ region) +
  labs(
    title = "The Same Association Split By Region",
    x = "Per-capita income, U.S. dollars, log scale",
    y = "Infant mortality per 1,000 live births"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Faceting makes within-region patterns easier to inspect, but it can make it harder to compare exact positions across all countries at once.

Takeaways

  • Scatterplots show association and spread at the same time.
  • Scale choices can make structure easier or harder to see.
  • Smoothers summarize patterns but should not replace the points.
  • Grouping can reveal structure, but it can also overload the plot.
  • Use association language unless the study design supports causal claims.
